beats.narration_text: Olá — this is Liam, in for Bear. Ask Claude to create a polished technical report as a Word document with a table of contents, headings, and page numbers. Before Claude writes a line of code, the docx skill fires. A dot-docx file is a ZIP archive containing XML. That is the insight. Let me show you the two paths.

props.command: Create a polished technical report as a Word document with TOC, headings, and page numbers.
output: Path 1 (create): docx-js — Document / Paragraph / Table / Packer
A .docx is a ZIP of XML. That is the insight.

beats.narration_text: The docx skill covers two paths. Create: use docx-js, an npm package, to generate a new document from JavaScript. Edit: unpack the ZIP, edit the XML files directly, and repack. The quick reference maps five task types to their approach — read with pandoc, create with docx-js, edit with unpack and repack, convert legacy dot-doc files with LibreOffice, and convert to images via PDF.

props.sparkLine: A .docx is just a ZIP of XML.

beats.narration_text: The create path has five critical rules — all fatal if you miss them. One: set page size explicitly — docx-js defaults to A4, not US Letter. Two: never use backslash-n for line breaks — use separate Paragraph elements. Three: never use unicode bullets — use LevelFormat.BULLET with a numbering config. Four: tables need dual widths — columnWidths on the table AND width on each cell, both in DXA units. Five: always use WidthType.DXA — never WidthType.PERCENTAGE, which silently breaks in Google Docs.

props.sparkLine: docx-js defaults to A4. Set US Letter explicitly.

beats.narration_text: The edit path is three steps in order. Step one: run unpack.py to extract the ZIP into XML files — it pretty-prints and merges adjacent runs. Step two: use the Edit tool with str-replace on the XML — do not write Python scripts; the Edit tool shows exactly what is being replaced. Step three: run pack.py to repack and validate. The XML has pitfalls: for tracked changes, replace the entire w-r element, not just the text inside it. The element order inside w-pPr is fixed — get it wrong and the XML is invalid.

props.sparkLine: Unpack. Edit XML. Repack. In that order.

beats.act: teardown — ZIP insight + what it gets right/bites
beats.narration_text: Here is the teardown moment. The architectural insight of the docx skill is that a Word document is just XML in a ZIP. That means the edit path requires no library — unpack it, edit the text, repack it. The create path documents five silent failure modes in docx-js: the defaults that produce wrong output with no error message. What it bites: docx-js defaults silently produce A4 documents. WidthType.PERCENTAGE tables break in Google Docs without warning. Tracked changes XML is verbose and easy to nest incorrectly.

props.sparkLine: It is XML. Treat it like XML.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. The docx skill is a two-path reference: docx-js for creating new documents, unpack-edit-repack for editing existing ones. Five docx-js rules are fatal if missed — all produce silent wrong output. For edits, the Edit tool with str-replace is the right approach, not Python scripts. The ZIP-is-XML insight removes the need for a library on the edit path.

artifactLines: Path 1 (create): docx-js — 5 fatal rules, all produce silent wrong output
Path 2 (edit): unpack → XML str_replace → repack — no library needed
Page size: always set explicitly — docx-js defaults to A4, not US Letter
Tables: WidthType.DXA only — PERCENTAGE silently breaks in Google Docs
Lists: LevelFormat.BULLET — never unicode bullets or \n
Edit path: Edit tool for str_replace — do not write Python scripts

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Open a Claude session. Paste this: I want to create a one-page technical memo as a Word document, US Letter format, with a header, heading styles, a two-column table, and a footer with page numbers. Use the docx skill. Then watch whether Claude sets page size explicitly and uses WidthType.DXA for the table. If it uses WidthType.PERCENTAGE, the table will break in Google Docs. That is the gate.

props.command: I want to create a one-page technical memo as a Word document, US Letter format, with a header, heading styles, a two-column table, and a footer with page numbers. Use the docx skill.
